metadata.summary: Handle paginated web content via next-button, infinite-scroll, and load-more patterns. Manage browser memory with page pooling and semaphores for concurrent scrapes.

content.title: Web Scraping — Pagination and Memory Management
content.summary: Three pagination patterns (next-button, infinite-scroll, load-more) cover virtually all paginated sites. Pair them with a browser pool or semaphore to bound memory when scraping many URLs concurrently.

why.p: Pagination is the most common source of incomplete scrape datasets. Each pattern has a specific termination condition; missing it produces either premature stops or infinite loops with duplicate rows. Memory leaks from unclosed pages are the second most common production failure in long-running scrapers.

list.item: Scraping a listing page that spans multiple pages via a next/prev button.
Extracting all items from a feed or social wall that uses infinite scroll.
Handling a "Load More" button that appends items to the same DOM without navigation.
Running concurrent scrapes across many URLs where browser memory must be bounded.
Single-page sites with no pagination — add unnecessary complexity.
APIs with offset/cursor parameters — use HTTP pagination, not DOM pagination.
When the total item count is small enough to fit in one DOM render — no loop needed.

rule.statement: For next-button pagination, loop until the next button is absent or disabled. For infinite scroll, break when document.body.scrollHeight stops growing. For load-more buttons, break when the button is absent or not in the viewport. Always deduplicate collected items by index or hash.
rule.rationale: Each termination condition maps to a different DOM state. Using the wrong check (e.g., "next button exists" when the button is disabled but still in DOM) produces infinite loops. Deduplication guards against the off-by-one overlap in infinite scroll.

example.title: Next-button pagination
Infinite scroll pagination
example.note: Always use allData.length as the slice offset — re-extracting all items and slicing prevents duplicate rows even when the DOM re-renders existing items.

example.title: Load-more button pagination

antipattern.description: Checking only "button exists" without checking disabled state or viewport visibility for next/load-more termination.
antipattern.reason: Many sites keep the button in the DOM but set it to disabled or visually hidden on the last page; the loop never terminates.

antipattern.description: In infinite scroll, re-collecting all items each iteration without slicing by previous count.
antipattern.reason: Produces duplicate rows every iteration because already-collected items are still in the DOM.

rule.statement: Always close pages in a finally block after use. For concurrent URL lists, use a semaphore or p-limit to cap open pages. Implement a BrowserPool when launching multiple browser instances to avoid unbounded memory growth.
rule.rationale: Each open Chromium page consumes 50–200 MB of RAM. A 100-URL batch without page management exhausts memory on any standard VPS within minutes.

example.title: Semaphore-bounded concurrent scraping
BrowserPool for multi-browser concurrency
Leaking pages without close in finally
example.note: Wrap page.close() in finally so it always runs, even on extraction errors.

antipattern.description: Launching a new browser per URL in a large batch instead of reusing a pool.
antipattern.reason: Browser launch adds 1–3 seconds of overhead and creates a new Chromium process for each URL; 100 URLs = 100 Chromium processes running simultaneously.
